scope_note: Static review covers nokogiri's own in-tree code: the C bridge (ext/nokogiri/*.c) and the Ruby API (lib/). The vendored native libraries (libxml2, libxslt, gumbo-parser, zlib, libiconv) are fetched/compiled at build time and are NOT in this checkout, so their memory-corruption surface — the dominant historical CVE source — is out of scope for static review. For execution-verified crashes in the native parsers, use vuln-pipeline.

findings.title: DEFAULT_XSLT enables NOENT and DTDLOAD, allowing entity/DTD loading when processing XSLT
findings.description: DEFAULT_XSLT = RECOVER | NONET | NOENT | DTDLOAD | DTDATTR | NOCDATA | BIG_LINES (parse_options.rb:342). Unlike DEFAULT_XML (line 334, which omits NOENT/DTDLOAD), the XSLT default substitutes entities and loads DTDs. A stylesheet or input document parsed under these options can reference external/parameter entities and DTDs; NONET blocks network fetches, but local file disclosure via SYSTEM entities / DTD load and entity-expansion DoS remain reachable. The code carries an explicit warning comment that this set is unsafe for untrusted documents.
findings.exploit_scenario: An application that compiles an attacker-influenced XSLT stylesheet (or transforms a document that pulls in a malicious DTD) under the default options has entities expanded and DTDs loaded, enabling local file read via SYSTEM entities or billion-laughs-style expansion.
findings.recommendation: Never parse untrusted XSLT. Offer/document a hardened XSLT option set without NOENT/DTDLOAD for cases where stylesheet trust cannot be guaranteed.
findings.confidence_reason: The unsafe flags are real and on-by-default for XSLT, but the behavior is documented with an explicit warning and entity loading is intrinsic to XSLT semantics; triage may rule intended-but-documented.

findings.title: XSLT/XPath parameter injection when transform() params are not quoted
findings.description: build_xslt_params (xslt_stylesheet.c:155-163) takes each Ruby param via StringValueCStr (line 160) and passes the raw C strings to xsltApplyStylesheet. libxslt interprets parameter VALUES as XPath expressions, not literals. If an application forwards untrusted input as a transform() parameter value without wrapping it in Nokogiri::XSLT.quote_params, the attacker controls an XPath expression evaluated inside the stylesheet context.
findings.exploit_scenario: App does stylesheet.transform(doc, {"q" => userInput}); attacker supplies an XPath payload as userInput that exfiltrates or alters selected nodes / influences output beyond the intended literal.
findings.recommendation: Always pass user-controlled params through Nokogiri::XSLT.quote_params; document that raw param values are XPath expressions.
findings.confidence_reason: Real injection class, but the library provides quote_params and documents the contract; reachability requires caller misuse, so medium-low.

findings.title: XPath injection via untrusted CSS/XPath selectors built by the caller
findings.description: The CSS->XPath translation validates function NAMES (validate_xpath_function_name, xpath_visitor.rb:304) but does not sanitize attacker-controlled selector VALUES. When an application builds a selector or XPath string by interpolating untrusted input and passes it to Node#search/#xpath/#css, the attacker can alter the query (XPath injection), reading nodes outside the intended scope.
findings.exploit_scenario: doc.xpath("//user[@name='#{params[:name]}']") with name = "' or '1'='1" returns all user nodes; richer payloads traverse to sibling/secret nodes.
findings.recommendation: Use variable bindings / parameterized XPath (doc.xpath(expr, nil, name: value)) instead of string interpolation; treat selectors built from untrusted input as injection-prone.
findings.confidence_reason: Primarily a caller-side misuse pattern; nokogiri offers parameterized XPath. Reportable as a candidate but likely a caller-responsibility / intended-API verdict in triage.

findings.title: CDATA length truncated by (int) cast of strlen in the gumbo bridge
findings.description: In the GUMBO_NODE_CDATA case, xmlNewCDataBlock is called with (int) strlen(gumbo_child->v.text.text) (gumbo.c:230-231). strlen returns size_t; the cast to int truncates (and can go negative) for text nodes larger than INT_MAX. For a >2GB CDATA section this passes a wrong/negative length to libxml2.
findings.exploit_scenario: A >2GB CDATA section in attacker HTML produces a truncated/negative length into xmlNewCDataBlock, potentially mis-sizing the copied buffer.
findings.recommendation: Validate the length fits in int (or use a size_t-aware API / cap node size) before the cast.
findings.confidence_reason: Requires a multi-gigabyte single text node; gumbo/host limits make this largely theoretical — defense-in-depth.
